narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 32, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3—and calculated a 68.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA. Your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish ranking, had the strongest push toward predicting bar failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, again much lower than the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often correlate with bar challenges. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing it as a relative strength in your profile. Overall, these academic rankings stood out as bigger red flags than your LSAT or family income because they deviated sharply from what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar. Compared to average applicants, your lower deciles put you at a clear disadvantage, as most successful bar takers rank higher in their school cohorts. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 36.7 average, strengthening your early grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting any improvements—these steps have helped many in similar situations turn predictions around.
